picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: Alphabet is the strongest Mag-7 performer year-to-date, with Q1 2026 EPS of $5.11 (+82% YoY) on revenues of $109.9B (+21.8% YoY), while Google Cloud surged 63% to $20B with backlog doubling to over $460B — a generational compounding engine. Druckenmiller increased his GOOGL position by 276.7% in Q4 2025, making it a top-10 holding and signaling renewed conviction in AI monetization, while Ackman trimmed his position after strong gains. The US-China tariff truce (May 12) and Trump-Xi summit reduce regulatory uncertainty and support global ad-spend recovery, lifting GOOGL's near-term revenue ceiling.
picks.catalyst: Google I/O developer conference (typically mid-May) and Q2 2026 earnings in late July — both are catalysts for cloud and AI monetization narrative step-ups.
picks.catalystWindow: Next 4–10 weeks
picks.entryNote: Stock up ~30% over 6 months and consolidating near all-time highs; the Q1 EPS beat was partially inflated by $37.7B in non-operating investment gains — strip those out and the operating multiple is approximately 22-24x forward earnings, still reasonable for a business with 19-21% revenue growth and the highest-quality AI infrastructure moat in advertising.
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud growth decelerates below 40% YoY, or Search revenue is materially disrupted by AI-native competitors (Perplexity, OpenAI SearchGPT) gaining measurable market share above 3-5% of query volume.
picks.keyRisk: AI-native search disruption reducing Search CPCs over a 2-3 year horizon — the single largest structural threat to 55%+ of revenue.

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: Broadcom is the single highest-quality AI semiconductor name in the universe — Q1 2026 revenue hit a record $19.3B (+29% YoY) with AI semiconductor revenue up 106% YoY, Q2 guidance projects AI chip revenue growth of 140% YoY, and FCF reached $8B (41% of revenue) at a 77% gross margin. Citi raised its price target to $500 on May 12, Wells Fargo raised to $545 on May 14, and BofA named AVGO a top semiconductor pick alongside AMD. The Trump-Xi summit, with Jensen Huang and Tim Cook in attendance, signals potential easing of chip export controls — a direct positive for AVGO's custom ASIC partnerships with hyperscalers including Apple, Google, and Meta.
picks.catalyst: Fiscal Q2 2026 earnings on June 3 — expected to confirm the 140% AI revenue guidance and potentially raise full-year custom silicon TAM estimates.
picks.catalystWindow: Next 3 weeks
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$435 on May 14, a new 52-week high; P/E of ~82x trailing but ~35-38x forward on FY2026 consensus — justified by the 77% gross margin, 41% FCF yield, and the clearest AI ASIC revenue ramp in the industry. Not cheap, but the quality overlay is the strongest of any AI name.
picks.exitTrigger: AI ASIC revenue growth decelerates below 60% YoY, gross margins compress below 73%, or a major hyperscaler publicly signals migration to in-house silicon and away from Broadcom custom chips.
picks.keyRisk: Heavy customer concentration — top 3 hyperscalers represent the majority of AI chip revenue; a single design win loss would be materially negative.

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: Goldman Sachs is the highest-quality beneficiary of the Tier 1 Financials overweight thesis — a steepening yield curve (2s10s at +51 bps and bear-steepening), recovering M&A and IPO activity, and deregulatory tailwinds under the current administration drive NIM expansion and fee income recovery. The US-China tariff truce meaningfully revives cross-border M&A flow and capital markets activity, where Goldman has the deepest franchise. Trading at ~12-13x forward earnings, near its 10-year median, with ROIC meaningfully above cost of equity — the quality/value combination is among the most attractive in the growth book.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings in mid-July — first full quarter reflecting both deregulatory pipeline and the post-tariff-truce M&A revival. Any Fed hike cycle also expands trading revenues.
picks.catalystWindow: Next 8-10 weeks
picks.entryNote: Carries over from prior sessions; the stock has been in the top 4 since May 5. Forward P/E near 10-year median (~13-14x) with earnings revision breadth positive — one of the cheapest Tier 1 names on the screen relative to quality.
picks.exitTrigger: Investment banking fee revenues decline more than 20% QoQ, credit spreads blow out above 500 bps HY OAS (signaling a credit cycle), or the yield curve re-inverts materially.
picks.keyRisk: A sudden credit cycle — if HY defaults spike and M&A pipelines freeze simultaneously, GS's trading and advisory revenues compress sharply from peak cycle levels.

picks.name: Eli Lilly and Company
picks.rationale: Eli Lilly dominates the GLP-1 franchise — a decade-long structural revenue story with Mounjaro/Zepbound still in early innings of global rollout. The GLP-1 market is projected to exceed $150B globally by 2030, and Lilly has been growing to become the largest holding in XLV at ~15% — reflecting institutional consensus on the durability of this franchise. In a stagflationary regime where consumer discretionary is penalized, healthcare/pharma with inelastic demand and pricing power is a natural overweight. The sector rotation analysis confirms Pharma/GLP-1 as a 'LEADER' sub-sector within healthcare.
picks.catalyst: Phase 3 readouts for oral GLP-1 (orforglipron) and renal/heart failure indications in mid-2026 — any positive data materially expands the addressable market beyond obesity/diabetes.
picks.catalystWindow: June–September 2026
picks.entryNote: Carries over from prior sessions as a persistent top-5 conviction name. LLY trades at ~35-38x forward EPS — a premium but defensible given a 25%+ revenue CAGR over the next 3 years and near-zero threat of generic competition on biologics for 10+ years.
picks.exitTrigger: Phase 3 trial failure for next-generation GLP-1 oral formulation, or unexpected FDA safety signal on tirzepatide that triggers a label restriction.
picks.keyRisk: Medicare drug price negotiation under IRA expanding to cover Mounjaro/Zepbound sooner than expected, compressing net pricing by 20-40%.

picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation
picks.rationale: ExxonMobil is the highest-quality energy major in the universe — the Iran war energy pass-through (Brent near $115, gasoline +28.4% YoY) has driven a structural earnings upgrade cycle, and XOM benefits from both upstream production and downstream refining/chemicals via the Pioneer acquisition. Berkshire Hathaway added to CVX (the closest peer) in Q4 2025 citing energy as a commodity inflation hedge. The sector rotation analysis upgraded Energy to Tier 1 on May 12 given $100+ oil and Iran escalation risk. Today's Trump-Xi summit discusses Iran as a secondary topic, meaning any diplomatic resolution is a tail risk but not the base case within a 1-3 year horizon.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings in late July — the first full quarter with $100+ Brent embedded in realized pricing, expected to show significant EPS upgrades and potentially accelerated buybacks.
picks.catalystWindow: Next 10-12 weeks
picks.entryNote: Carries over at high conviction from May 5. XOM trades at ~13-14x forward earnings — a 20-25% discount to the S&P 500 — with a 3.4% dividend yield providing downside cushion. Energy sector intraweek cooling noted in May 14 update; trimmed from #3 to #5 on the growth book to reflect tactical cooling while maintaining the structural thesis.
picks.exitTrigger: Brent crude sustainably falls below $75/barrel (Iran peace deal + Saudi production increase + demand destruction from recession), compressing upstream margins below the 2023 trough.
picks.keyRisk: A rapid Iran ceasefire agreement — while geopolitically positive, it would remove the war risk premium from crude pricing and trigger a sharp sector re-rating.

picks.name: RTX Corporation (Raytheon Technologies)
picks.rationale: RTX is the dominant defense/aerospace prime with the best combination of missile systems (Raytheon), commercial aerospace (Pratt & Whitney, Collins Aerospace), and geopolitical optionality — the Iran war has generated a multi-year order book surge that is still translating into backlog growth. The macro regime briefing specifically cites defense primes (LMT, RTX, NOC) as clear beneficiaries in the 'what's working' section, and margins in defense primes are expanding per the May 13 sector update. Unlike LMT or NOC, RTX has the commercial aerospace lever as a second engine of growth as air travel recovers.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings in late July — backlog update and Pratt & Whitney GTF engine repair resolution expected to clarify the revenue/margin trajectory for the commercial segment.
picks.catalystWindow: Next 10-12 weeks
picks.entryNote: Carries over as a persistent top-7 name since May 5 at high conviction. RTX trades at approximately 20-22x forward EPS, near its 5-year average, with a defense backlog providing 3+ years of revenue visibility — an unusual combination of growth and earnings predictability.
picks.exitTrigger: Iran conflict ends via negotiated peace with rapid de-escalation, AND Pratt & Whitney GTF engine repair costs exceed $8B in additional charges (already partially reserved), compressing aerospace segment margins.
picks.keyRisk: Continuing resolution or US defense budget sequestration that delays Raytheon missile system deliveries, combined with a commercial aerospace demand softening.

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.
picks.rationale: Amazon Web Services re-acceleration to +28% YoY (its fastest growth in 15 quarters) in Q1 2026 — with $37.6B in revenue — is the single most important data point: cloud is the AI infrastructure backbone and AMZN is a direct beneficiary of hyperscaler capex compounding. The tariff truce (US-China, May 12) meaningfully reduces e-commerce margin headwinds from tariff-sensitive supply chains. FCF deterioration from heavy capex is a known near-term headwind, but the operating story (AWS, advertising) is structurally intact. Berkshire reduced AMZN by 77% in Q4 2025, which is a yellow flag but reflects Buffett's historically poor timing on tech exits.
picks.catalyst: AWS growth trajectory in Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — any acceleration above 30% would be a significant positive revision catalyst for the AWS DCF.
picks.catalystWindow: Next 10-12 weeks
picks.entryNote: Carries over from prior sessions. Stock up ~15% over 6 months vs. SPY; Q1 EPS beat of $1.10 vs. consensus was partly aided by a $16.8B Anthropic gain, but AWS operating income growth is the real signal. Forward P/E approximately 33-35x — within historical range for a business with AWS/advertising growing 25-30%.
picks.exitTrigger: AWS growth decelerates below 20% YoY for two consecutive quarters, or e-commerce operating margins compress below 5% due to labor/fulfillment cost inflation.
picks.keyRisk: Heavy capex cycle ($80B+ in 2026 and 2027) depresses FCF and forces multiple re-rating if cloud growth disappoints; the Anthropic investment gains are not repeatable.

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: S&P Global is a best-in-class financial information oligopoly — the Ratings and Market Intelligence segments benefit from both a higher-for-longer rate environment (triggering massive issuance as companies refinance before potential hike cycles) and a recovering capital markets backdrop. In a stagflationary regime, SPGI's pricing power — it raises fees annually with near-zero customer churn — acts as a natural inflation hedge. ROIC exceeds 25%, margins are above 45%, and the business requires virtually zero incremental capital to grow. Persistent top-10 name in the book since May 6.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings in late July — debt issuance volumes are a direct input; rising rate expectations should drive pull-forward issuance and lift Ratings revenue above consensus.
picks.catalystWindow: Next 10-12 weeks
picks.entryNote: Carries over from May 6 at high conviction. SPGI trades at approximately 27-29x forward EPS — modest premium to historical average of ~25x — with a ~1% dividend yield and consistent 10-12% EPS CAGR over the past decade. Not cheap on a P/E basis but justified by the near-monopoly economics.
picks.exitTrigger: Debt issuance volumes collapse more than 30% YoY due to a credit market freeze, OR antitrust action forces a structural breakup of Ratings and Market Intelligence.
picks.keyRisk: Regulatory intervention in the credit rating oligopoly — any mandated competition from new entrants or government-backed rating agencies would structurally impair the Ratings segment moat.

picks.name: Vistra Energy Corp.
picks.rationale: Vistra is the standout quality/momentum play within power generation — the AI data center electricity supercycle is a 10-year structural demand story, and VST holds the largest independent power producer position in the US with firm power contracts being aggressively sought by hyperscalers. The sector rotation analysis confirmed Utilities as a 'data center power play' with AI demand creating a secular re-rating. Positive estimate revisions span Utilities per the May 13 sector update. The stock has more than doubled from its 52-week low, reflecting an institutional consensus that has already formed — hence the medium conviction and reduced weight.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings and any announcement of long-term power purchase agreements with hyperscalers (Microsoft, Google, Amazon) — these contracts lock in 10-15 year demand visibility.
picks.catalystWindow: Next 8-12 weeks
picks.entryNote: Carries over from prior sessions but weight reduced from 'hi' to 'me' and suggestedWeight trimmed to 5% to reflect the May 14 sector update flagging Energy cooling and VST's extended technical position. The AI power thesis is widely owned; entry here is on structural conviction, not momentum.
picks.exitTrigger: Data center buildout timelines slip by 12+ months, reducing near-term power demand; OR interest rates rise above 5.5% on the 10-year, compressing utility sector multiples.
picks.keyRisk: A sharp rate spike — as a capital-intensive asset with long-duration cash flows, VST is more interest-rate sensitive than the growth names in this book despite its growth profile.

picks.name: Arista Networks Inc.
picks.rationale: Arista is the #1 market share holder in high-speed switching (>10 Gigabit Ethernet), reporting Q1 2026 revenue of $2.709B (+35.1% YoY) with EPS of $0.87 beating the $0.79 estimate by 10.1%. Management raised full-year 2026 revenue guidance to ~$11.5B and lifted the AI networking target to $3.5B from $3.25B. The Trump-Xi trade truce and Jensen Huang's participation in the Beijing summit signal potential easing of chip/networking export controls that could reopen the China hyperscaler market for ANET products — a meaningful upside optionality layer. Morgan Stanley and Barclays have both raised price targets recently.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings and any positive update from the Trump-Xi summit on AI networking export control relaxation — either would be a material positive revision catalyst.
picks.catalystWindow: Next 6-10 weeks
picks.entryNote: Reduced weight to medium/5% from prior sessions given the 'moderate extended' flag (stock has appreciated 61% per year over 3 years vs. 27% EPS CAGR) and the post-earnings -14.81% drop in the most recent quarter showing the market prices in perfection. Entry is at a tactical consolidation point above the 50dma.
picks.exitTrigger: Gross margin compression below 62%, evidence of supply constraints limiting revenue delivery, or loss of a major hyperscaler switching design win to a Cisco/Juniper alternative.
picks.keyRisk: Stock has historically priced in perfection — any guide that implies slowing AI networking spend or missed delivery timelines carries 15-20% downside risk in a single session.

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares ETF
picks.rationale: Gold is the only asset class providing pure regime-break insurance in a stagflationary environment — it rises in both inflationary shocks AND deflationary crises. With the Iran war driving WTI implied volatility to 51%+, CPI reaccelerating to 3.8% YoY, a 30% probability of a Fed rate hike by year-end (per CME data), and the Trump-Xi summit creating near-term risk-on but unresolved structural geopolitical tension, GLD is the cleanest tail hedge in the book. Central bank demand from China, India, and Russia provides a structural floor that did not exist in prior decades. Carries over at high conviction from May 5.
picks.catalyst: April PCE inflation print (~May 30) and any Fed hawkish pivot communication — both are direct positive catalysts as they reinforce the 'higher for longer / hike risk' narrative that gold thrives in.
picks.catalystWindow: Next 2-4 weeks
picks.entryNote: Gold at all-time highs in 2026; not a value entry, but a structural insurance entry. In a book 78% weighted toward risk-on growth equities, a 8% GLD allocation is a portfolio construction imperative, not a macro call alone. Historical drawdown in risk-off events is minimal vs. equity beta.
picks.exitTrigger: Fed initiates a sustained rate cut cycle below 3.0% with credible inflation defeat (core PCE below 2.2% for 3 consecutive months) AND geopolitical risks normalize — in that scenario, the opportunity cost of holding GLD vs. equities rises sharply.
picks.keyRisk: A rapid Iran ceasefire AND US-China trade resolution could temporarily compress the geopolitical risk premium embedded in gold, leading to a 5-10% pullback from all-time highs.

picks.name: Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD provides quality dividend exposure — fortress-balance-sheet companies with long dividend histories (Abbvie, Chevron, Coca-Cola, Verizon) — at roughly 15-16x forward earnings vs. the S&P 500's ~21x. Yielding ~3.5%, it provides income that competes with short-duration credit while maintaining equity upside. In a stagflationary regime where consumer purchasing power is being eroded (real wages -0.5% MoM in April), dividend payers with pricing power outperform by preserving real income for shareholders. Carries over as a persistent defensive sleeve anchor from May 5.
picks.catalyst: Any risk-off episode triggered by a hawkish Fed communication or geopolitical escalation — SCHD historically outperforms by 400-600 bps vs. SPY during drawdowns above 10%.
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing structural hold; no specific near-term catalyst needed
picks.entryNote: At a 25-30% valuation discount to the S&P 500, SCHD provides the cheapest quality equity exposure in the defensive sleeve. The 3.5% yield provides carry that T-bills can no longer offer after the rate hold.
picks.exitTrigger: A sustained bull market with S&P 500 earnings multiple compression toward 15x (in a Volcker-style rate shock) — in that scenario, even quality dividend payers reprice lower, and cash becomes the better defensive allocation.
picks.keyRisk: Rising rates compressing dividend equity multiples — if the 10-year rises to 5.5%+ on a hike cycle, SCHD's ~3.5% yield looks inadequate vs. risk-free alternatives.

picks.name: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. Class B
picks.rationale: Berkshire holds a record ~$380B in cash and short-term Treasuries, clipping $5B+ annually in T-bill income while maintaining a call option on dislocations — Greg Abel's first major move was exiting the Kraft Heinz position (cleaning the slate) and completing the $9.5B OxyChem acquisition (real-asset inflation hedge). In four of the last four major risk-off events, Berkshire meaningfully outperformed the S&P 500 on drawdown. At ~1.5x book value, it is structurally cheap for the embedded quality. Q1 2026 13F filing due May 15 — any cash deployment signal would be a positive catalyst for the stock itself. Carries over from May 8.
picks.catalyst: Q1 2026 13F filing on May 15 — the market will scrutinize whether Abel deployed any of the $380B cash hoard into the tariff-driven Q1 drawdown, which would itself be a buy signal for BRK.B.
picks.catalystWindow: Tomorrow (May 15) — 13F filing date
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~1.5x book — historically reasonable for Berkshire; the cash position alone (earning ~5% on Treasuries) provides a natural floor. Not a momentum name but a portfolio ballast with option value.
picks.exitTrigger: Abel deploys the cash hoard into overpriced acquisitions or begins consistently underperforming SPY on both an up-market and down-market basis — signaling loss of capital allocation discipline in the post-Buffett era.
picks.keyRisk: Transition risk under Abel — if the first major acquisition under his leadership is a capital-destroying overpayment (similar to the Kraft Heinz mistake under Buffett), sentiment toward the stock could turn sharply negative.

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF
picks.rationale: IEF (7-10 year duration) provides the cleaner Treasury hedge vs. TLT in the current environment — enough duration to rally meaningfully in a recession scare or Fed pivot, without the fiscal/supply risk that has crushed the 30-year end. The 10-year currently yields ~4.39%, offering genuine real yield pickup from cash (above the ~2.8% core CPI) while preserving capital if the growth slowdown deepens. With Q2 GDPNow tracking +3.7%, the recession scenario is not imminent, but IEF is the cheapest insurance policy against a growth reversal and positions the book to benefit from any forced Fed pivot.
picks.catalyst: April PCE print (~May 30) or any Fed communication signaling concern about growth — either would rally IEF as the market re-prices the rate path lower.
picks.catalystWindow: Next 2-6 weeks
picks.entryNote: At 4.39% yield on the 10-year, IEF offers the best risk-adjusted entry in fixed income since 2007 — carry + potential capital gain in a slowdown vs. the equity beta of the rest of the book. Sized at 4% as a genuine hedge, not a significant return driver.
picks.exitTrigger: 10-year Treasury yields breach 5.25% on a sustained basis (indicating a hike cycle has begun) — in that case, IEF duration becomes a liability and the allocation should rotate to T-bills or short-duration IG credit.
picks.keyRisk: A sustained Fed hike cycle — if the 39% hike odds priced by markets materialize and the Fed hikes twice in late 2026/early 2027, IEF faces 8-10% price drawdown before the carry offsets the capital loss.

picks.name: The Coca-Cola Company
picks.rationale: Coca-Cola is the cleanest consumer staples defensive in the universe — pricing power embedded in 200+ country distribution, 60%+ gross margins, and a 3.2% dividend yield backed by 62 consecutive years of dividend growth. In a stagflationary regime with real wages declining (-0.5% MoM in April 2026), KO's brand-driven pricing power allows it to pass through cost inflation without volume destruction, while lower-income consumer stress (K-shaped consumption) drives trade-down to affordable beverage brands. Berkshire Hathaway holds KO at ~10.2% of the disclosed equity portfolio — the longest-held position in the Buffett book — a testament to its moat durability. Carries over from May 7.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings in late July — organic revenue growth and pricing/volume split will confirm whether tariff-driven input cost pass-through is working without volume elasticity damage.
picks.catalystWindow: Next 10-12 weeks
picks.entryNote: KO trades at ~22-24x forward earnings — slightly above its 10-year median of ~21x — but justified by the dividend growth history and near-zero macro sensitivity. The 3.2% yield provides an anchor in a volatile rate environment.
picks.exitTrigger: Volume declines exceed 3% in two consecutive quarters in North America (indicating brand elasticity failure), OR a major sugar/sweetener cost shock compresses gross margins below 55%.
picks.keyRisk: GLP-1 drugs (Mounjaro, Wegovy) reducing caloric consumption structurally — a multi-year headwind if GLP-1 adoption reaches 15%+ of the US adult population and sugar beverage volumes fall faster than price can offset.

summary: The macro regime remains stagflationary-adjacent — CPI at 3.8% YoY (3-year high), PPI at +6.0% YoY, real wages -0.5% MoM, and 30-39% market-implied odds of a Fed rate hike by year-end — but a significant new positive development arrived today: the US-China tariff truce (May 12, tariffs cut from 145% to 30%) and the Trump-Xi Beijing summit (May 14) have sparked a broad market rally with the S&P 500 recovering all Liberation Day losses, and NVIDIA's CEO Jensen Huang joining the summit to negotiate AI chip export controls. The 10-pick growth book expresses the dominant regime themes — AI infrastructure compounding (GOOGL, AVBO, ANET), stagflation beneficiaries with pricing power (XOM, SPGI, LLY), defense/geopolitical hedge (RTX), financials benefiting from the steepening curve and M&A revival (GS), and the AI power demand supercycle (VST) — while AMZN captures the AWS re-acceleration. The defensive sleeve (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO) provides genuine tail-risk insurance: GLD hedges the inflation/hike scenario, SCHD and KO provide quality income at valuation discounts, BRK.B provides cash-deployment optionality with the critical Q1 13F filing due tomorrow, and IEF hedges the growth-reversal scenario. The dominant risk being underwritten is that the Trump-Xi summit fails to deliver on export control relaxation for AI chips, the Iran war re-escalates, and a stagflation-into-recession outcome forces simultaneous equity multiple compression and credit spread widening.
diversificationNote: The growth book spans 6 distinct GICS sectors — Communication Services (GOOGL), Information Technology (AVBO, ANET), Financials (GS, SPGI), Health Care (LLY), Energy (XOM), Industrials (RTX), Consumer Discretionary (AMZN), and Utilities (VST) — with at most 2 names from any single sector; the two IT names (AVBO custom AI ASICs, ANET AI networking) share the AI infrastructure theme but occupy different points in the value chain (silicon vs. switching). The defensive sleeve spans 5 distinct exposures: gold (GLD), broad dividend equity (SCHD), capital-allocation conglomerate/cash fortress (BRK.B), intermediate-duration Treasuries (IEF), and consumer staples (KO) — with no double-counting of any single exposure type.

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: Alphabet delivered Q1 2026 EPS of $5.11 — a +93.6% beat vs. consensus — on 21.8% revenue growth, with Google Cloud surging 63% to $20B and Cloud backlog nearly doubling to $462B. The AI monetization flywheel (Search AI Overviews, Gemini embedded in Workspace, Cloud AI workloads) is compounding into the world's deepest distribution network while forward estimates have been aggressively revised upward. Ackman, Viking Global, Tiger Global, and Coatue all hold GOOGL as a core position, providing heavyweight institutional validation.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) with continued Cloud re-acceleration and AI Overviews monetization data; April CPI print May 12 as a benign macro relief catalyst
picks.catalystWindow: Next 4 weeks (May 12 CPI) + next 2 quarters (Q2 earnings)
picks.entryNote: Trading ~$337, up ~30% over 6 months vs. SPY; forward P/E of approximately 20x for a business growing cloud at 63% is deeply undemanding relative to peers — not technically extended given the Q1 earnings reset higher
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud growth decelerates below 30% YoY for two consecutive quarters, or regulatory breakup order targeting Search materially advances in courts
picks.keyRisk: AI-driven search disruption (Perplexity, OpenAI SearchGPT) eroding query volume and CPCs faster than Cloud offsets

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: Broadcom's Q1 FY2026 beat of +9% vs. estimates on 29% revenue growth (semiconductor segment +52%) reflects structurally durable demand for custom AI ASICs from Google, Meta, and ByteDance — the ASIC TAM is the most defensible moat in semiconductors. VMware's infrastructure software suite layered onto the chip business produces high-margin recurring revenue that insulates earnings in a cyclical downturn. 48 of 50 analysts rate it a Buy. Today's dip on OpenAI chip-deal financing noise ($418 vs. 52-week high $437) is a tactical entry opportunity rather than a thesis break, as the OpenAI relationship is a marginal contributor — the hyperscaler ASIC engine (Google TPU, Meta MTIA) is the core.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2026 earnings (est. June 5, 2026) — next formal update on AI ASIC backlog and VMware integration margins
picks.catalystWindow: Next 4 weeks for post-noise stabilization; June 5 earnings event
picks.entryNote: Trading ~$418 on May 8, pulled ~4% intraday on OpenAI deal financing headlines — 52-week range $203–$437; forward P/E ~43x on 25%+ growth is premium but justified given VMware recurring revenue contribution; entry here is meaningfully below ATH
picks.exitTrigger: Loss of a second major hyperscaler ASIC design win (beyond OpenAI noise) or VMware customer churn accelerating above 10% annually
picks.keyRisk: OpenAI custom chip financing difficulties signal a broader risk that non-hyperscaler ASIC customers lack balance sheet depth to fund $10B+ chip programs, reducing future ASIC TAM

picks.name: Meta Platforms Inc.
picks.rationale: Meta's Q1 2026 revenue grew 33% YoY with record operating margins as AI-driven ad ranking tools (Andromeda, Advantage+) continue extracting higher CPMs from the same inventory base — a monetization flywheel requiring zero incremental distribution cost. Management's capex raise post-Q1 signals confidence in monetization ROI rather than speculative spending. Bill Ackman initiated META as an 11.4% portfolio position in Q4 2025 — his largest new buy in years — citing the AI-ad flywheel and dominant platform moat.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) with AI ad monetization metrics and Reality Labs cost discipline; ongoing AI advertising tool rollout to SMBs via Meta Advantage+
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing (AI ad product cycle) + Q2 earnings late July
picks.entryNote: Gross margins above 80%, FCF generation elite; forward P/E approximately 25x for 30%+ revenue growth implies a PEG ratio below 1.0 — manageable multiple for the quality of compounding; technically consolidating above prior resistance
picks.exitTrigger: Two consecutive quarters of ad revenue growth decelerating below 15%, or regulatory break-up of WhatsApp/Instagram from Facebook core
picks.keyRisk: Generative AI-native social platforms (character.ai, TikTok successors) capture Gen-Z engagement at scale, structurally reducing Meta's time-on-app metrics

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: Goldman Sachs is the highest-beta expression of the macro regime's most favorable impulses: a steepening yield curve (+50bp 10Y/2Y spread), HY OAS near historically tight 277bp supporting active M&A and IPO deal pipelines, and capital markets revenue surging as the Iran War creates advisory demand in energy, defense, and geopolitical M&A. The Financials sector (XLF) received Druckenmiller's single largest buy in Q4 2025, and Lone Pine Capital holds Financials as its largest sector allocation. Record blended S&P margins at 13.4% also support GS's asset/wealth management fee revenue.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (mid-July) with investment banking revenue disclosures — IPO/M&A advisory backlog is the key metric given elevated deal activity
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing (deal market open) + Q2 earnings mid-July
picks.entryNote: Financials sector trades at a discount to the index per the macro research; GS at roughly 13x forward earnings vs. historical 11-14x range is fair-to-attractive for a capital markets cycle leader; has been a consistent top-5 hold since May 4
picks.exitTrigger: HY OAS widens above 450bp (stress signal), 10Y/2Y yield curve inverts again, or Q2 investment banking revenue declines more than 20% QoQ
picks.keyRisk: Iran War escalation triggers a risk-off credit event that freezes deal markets and widens spreads sharply — GS is the most direct casualty in that scenario

picks.name: Eli Lilly and Company
picks.rationale: Eli Lilly's tirzepatide franchise (Mounjaro/Zepbound) is structurally independent of the AI capex cycle and Iran War inflation shock — demand for GLP-1 weight-loss and diabetes therapy vastly exceeds supply through at least 2027. The regulatory moat (clinical data superiority, manufacturing scale barriers, early-2030s patent protection) makes this a multi-decade compounder. phaseData.macro:
Here is your full macro regime briefing as of **Friday, May 8, 2026**:

---

# 🏦 U.S. EQUITY MACRO REGIME BRIEFING — MAY 8, 2026

---

## 📊 THE DATA DASHBOARD

### Fed Policy

At its April 29 meeting, the FOMC left the federal funds target range at **3.50%–3.75%**.
 
The Fed acknowledged that Middle East developments have increased uncertainty around the economic outlook and said officials are watching risks to both inflation and employment.
 
The decision kept the Fed in a wait-and-see posture; nearly all voting members supported holding rates steady, while one favored a 0.25% rate cut, and three others disagreed with the statement's easing bias rather than the rate decision itself — showing a divide over how strongly the Fed should signal potential future cuts.
 
The Fed's median projection (March dot plot) still points to **one quarter-point cut in 2026**.
 
The March 2026 dot plot projected a 3.00%–3.25% Fed funds rate by year-end 2027.
 
Core PCE inflation stood at **3.0%** in February 2026, well above the 2% target.
 With the EFFR at 3.625% (midpoint) and the neutral rate (r*) widely estimated at 2.5–3.0%, the **real policy rate is mildly restrictive** — but the geopolitical energy shock is complicating the calculus.

---

### Yield Curve

Long-end yields drifted lower at month-end, with the **10-year easing to 4.39%** and the **30-year settling at 4.97%**.
 The 2-year is implied near ~3.90% given the prevailing spread data. 
The 10-year/2-year spread is **+0.50pp** and the 10-year/3-month spread is **+0.75pp**.
 
Breakeven inflation expectations ticked higher: the 5-year breakeven rose to **2.72%** and the 10-year breakeven climbed to **2.50%**, suggesting markets are pricing in a small upward bias on inflation expectations as the next FOMC meeting approaches.
 The curve is positively sloped but **flat** — not a recessionary signal, but term premium direction is being pushed higher by energy-driven inflation worries on the long end.

---

### Inflation

In March, CPI for all urban consumers rose **0.9% MoM** and **+3.3% YoY**, while the core CPI (all items less food and energy) increased **0.2% MoM** and **+2.6% YoY**.
 
Over the year through March 2026, the headline PCE price index was **3.5%**, 1.1 percentage points above the 2.4% pace over the year through March 2025, with fluctuations in energy prices a large contributor to the pickup.
 
CPI inflation for energy goods and services averaged **3.2% per month in Q1 2026**, and on a 12-month basis, energy price inflation was **+12.5%** through March 2026, contrasting with a 3.3% decline a year earlier.
 
The ISM Services Prices Paid index held at **70.7** in April 2026 — the highest two-month average since October 2022, with all 18 industries tracked by the ISM reporting higher input costs.
 The inflation trajectory is clearly **re-accelerating**, driven by the energy/geopolitical shock; core is stickier than the Fed would like but not yet runaway.

---

### Growth

As of **May 8**, the Atlanta Fed GDPNow model estimates real GDP growth (SAAR) for Q2 2026 at **+3.7%**, with the nowcast of real gross private domestic investment growth rising to 9.2%, partly offset by a slight decline in personal consumption expenditures growth to 2.6%.
 
The first BEA estimate of Q1 real GDP growth came in at **+2.0%**, 0.8pp above GDPNow's final Q1 nowcast.
 
April nonfarm payrolls came in at **+115K** (well above consensus of ~62K), with gains concentrated in health care (+37K), transportation and warehousing (+30K), and retail trade (+22K).
 
The ISM Manufacturing PMI held at **52.7** in April 2026, matching its highest level since August 2022.
 
The ISM Services PMI inched down to **53.6** in April from 54 in March, loosely aligned with expectations but remaining firmly above prior-year averages.
 
The services prices gauge held at 70.7, with companies citing higher fuel, diesel, copper, and freight costs due to the war, plus aluminum and lumber costs from tariffs.


---

### Credit & Liquidity

ICE BofA US HY OAS was **~277 bps** (2.77%) in May 2026.
 This is extraordinarily tight by historical standards (long-run average ~500 bps), signaling risk-on complacency — or genuine credit quality at cycle highs. 
The 2026 credit market outlook calls for a rangebound rate environment and a modest increase in spreads within corporate credit markets; markets expect the Fed to lower the funds rate toward 3%, keeping the 10-year Treasury yield between 3.75%–4.25%.
 
Worker wages continue to outpace inflation, despite elevated price levels associated with the Iranian conflict.
 The DXY is showing modest weakness reflecting the Fed's on-hold posture and safe-haven demand for energy-importing currencies.

---

### Earnings Backdrop

With 63% of S&P 500 companies having reported Q1 2026, **84%** have beaten EPS estimates — above the 5-year average of 78% and the 10-year average of 76%.
 
In aggregate, companies are reporting earnings **20.7% above estimates**, which would mark the highest surprise percentage since Q1 2021 (22.2%).
 
The blended net profit margin for Q1 2026 stood at **13.4%** — a new record since FactSet began tracking the metric in 2009, surpassing the prior record of 13.2% set in Q4 2025.
 
The forward 12-month P/E ratio is **20.9×**, above the 5-year average (19.9×) and above the 10-year average (18.9×).
 
For CY 2026, analysts are predicting year-over-year earnings growth of **+21.3%**.
 
The Q2 bottom-up EPS estimate increased 2.1% (to **$80.47 from $78.84**) from March 31 to April 30 — an unusual positive revision in the first month of a quarter.
 
The S&P 500 is trading at approximately **$7,327** as of May 8, 2026.


---

### Geopolitical / Event Tape

According to ISM's April survey commentary, sentiment remained mixed in the second month of the Iran War, with 69% of comments negative, and the war mentioned in 47% of responses alongside tariffs in 18%.
 
Geopolitical uncertainty remains an upside risk to the medium-term inflation outlook.
 
Kevin Warsh, the next Fed Chair nominee, previously supported reducing interest rates
 — creating a leadership transition wildcard for H2 2026 monetary policy.

---

## 📋 THE THREE PARAGRAPHS

---

### 1. 🔴 Regime Call: **Late-Cycle / Stagflationary Overhang**

This is a **late-cycle regime with an embedded stagflationary shock** — not yet contraction, but structurally more fragile than mid-2025. 
The FOMC left rates at 3.50%–3.75%
, sitting above consensus neutral (~2.75–3.00%), meaning real policy rates are mildly restrictive even as nominal growth remains elevated. 
Headline PCE has re-accelerated to 3.5% YoY
 while 
the labor market is gradually cooling but remaining broadly resilient.
 The key tension: 
the ISM Services Prices Paid index held at 70.7 in April
 — the classic late-cycle stagflationary fingerprint of cost-push inflation running ahead of output deceleration. The Fed is trapped: it cannot ease without stoking inflation, and 
federal government employment continued to decline
 while manufacturing employment is contracting, narrowing the Fed's ability to declare mission accomplished. We are in a **late-cycle expansion with an energy-shock overlay** — bullish earnings momentum has extended the runway, but the clock is ticking.

---

### 2. ✅ What's Working / ❌ What's Breaking

**Working:**
- 
**Information Technology, Communication Services, Financials, and Real Estate** are all reporting year-over-year revenue growth
, and 
Alphabet, Amazon, and Meta were the three largest contributors to index earnings growth, dominating Q1 upside.
 AI capex beneficiaries (semis, cloud infrastructure, power) remain the dominant alpha sources.
- 
Record blended net profit margins at 13.4%
 favor **quality compounders** — companies with pricing power and lean cost structures, particularly in Tech and Industrials.
- **Energy** sector: 
12-month energy price inflation at +12.5%
 is a direct tailwind for integrated oils, LNG exporters, and defense-adjacent energy infrastructure.
- 
HY OAS near 277 bps
 signals benign credit conditions — **IG credit** and **leveraged finance** markets remain open for business.

**Breaking:**
- 
Manufacturing employment is declining at the sharpest pace in four months (ISM Employment sub-index at 46.4)
, and 
services employment contracted for a second consecutive month.

- 
New orders in Services sank 7.1 points to 53.5 — the sharpest decrease in three years — as consumer order volumes were dented by the surge in prices since the start of the war.

- 
Valuations now embed an expectation that the earnings trajectory continues, and the market's selective reaction to Big Tech capital spending plans suggests investors are no longer giving open-ended credit for AI investment without evidence of return.

- **Consumer discretionary** and **small-cap / high-beta cyclicals**: with 
5-year breakeven inflation at 2.72%
 and the Fed on hold, rate-sensitive sectors face continued headwinds. Rate cuts look increasingly H2 2026 at best.

---

### 3. 👁️ What to Watch: The 3 Highest-Impact Catalysts

1. **April CPI (May 12, 8:30am ET) — #1 Priority.** 
The Consumer Price Index for April 2026 is scheduled to be released on May 12.
 The market must resolve whether the energy shock's 0.9% MoM March print was a one-off or is feeding into core. If April core CPI surprises above ~0.3% MoM, the one remaining Fed cut in the dot plot gets repriced away entirely, long-end yields spike, and the 20.9× forward P/E becomes untenable. A benign print (~0.2% core) reopens the easing narrative and would be a relief catalyst.

2. **Iran War / Middle East Escalation Trajectory — Ongoing.** 
The Iran War was mentioned in 47% of ISM Manufacturing respondent comments in April
, making it the single largest driver of input cost uncertainty. Any material ceasefire or de-escalation would likely cause a rapid reversal of the energy inflation shock, collapsing the stagflation narrative. Conversely, Strait of Hormuz disruption or expansion of the conflict would accelerate the re-pricing of the Fed's neutral rate higher and trigger spread widening from historically tight levels.

3. **Fed Chair Transition (Kevin Warsh Confirmation Process) — 1-3 Month Horizon.** 
Kevin Warsh, the next Fed Chair nominee, previously supported reducing interest rates
, but he is also known as a market structuralist with hawkish instincts on inflation credibility. His confirmation hearings will be parsed intensely for any divergence from Powell's current "wait-and-see" stance. A dovish signal could reprice front-end rates down 25-50bps and provide a meaningful multiple expansion catalyst; a hawkish lean — particularly on fiscal dominance risks — could reverse the April ATH rally.

---

> **Bottom Line for the Portfolio Manager:** This is a regime that rewards **quality, pricing power, and earnings visibility** — not leverage, duration, or speculation. The S&P 500's new all-time high near 7,382 is fundamentally justified by record margins and a 27%+ Q1 blended earnings growth rate, but the 20.9× forward P/E leaves no margin for error on either the inflation or the geopolitical front. Stay long the compounders; hedge the energy shock via integrated oil exposure; and resist chasing small-cap cyclicals until the ISM employment sub-indices re-enter expansion territory.

Core PPI (ex-food and energy) accelerated **+1.0% MoM** vs. the +0.4% estimate.
 Critically, this isn't just an oil story: 
the services index surged **+1.2%** — its biggest gain since March 2022 — with two-thirds of the move attributed to a +2.7% gain in trade services, suggesting tariff pass-through is broadening.


**🏦 Fed Policy & Market Implications**

The prior analysis's conclusion that rate cuts are off the table in 2026 is now firmly validated — and the risk has shifted meaningfully toward hikes. 
Market pricing points to little chance of any rate cuts through the rest of the year, with odds for a **rate hike** climbing to approximately **39%** following the PPI report.
 
Notably, the April 29 FOMC meeting already saw **four dissents** — the highest since 1992 — with Governor Miran voting for a cut and three regional presidents objecting to dovish forward guidance language.
 The combined CPI + PPI prints this week represent a material upside inflation surprise and reinforce a **stagflationary** tilt: inflation re-accelerating into the 3.8–6.0% range on headline metrics, while 
payroll employment in April came in at only **+115,000** — a softening labor market.
 The macro regime has shifted from "mildly restrictive hold" toward a genuine **higher-for-longer** posture with non-trivial hike risk on the horizon.

The big development since Monday's briefing is the **April CPI print**, released Tuesday, May 12. It came in hotter than expected and materially changes the near-term policy outlook.

gas prices are up **28.4% YoY**

 per Morgan Stanley's Ellen Zentner.

 — signaling growing internal tension.
